unit.name: SimCLR, MoCo, CPC, and DINO in the modern SSL landscape
unit.description: Surveys major contrastive and near-contrastive methods, focusing on SimCLR's design choices and MoCo's queue-based alternative, then briefly situates CPC and DINO.
unit.summary: SimCLR is introduced as an instance-contrastive method that creates two augmented views per image, encodes both, and uses all other non-positive samples in the batch as negatives, so a batch of N images yields 2N views and a positive partner for each view [ts=3976s] [ts=4104s]. The lecture highlights SimCLR's empirical impact—linear evaluation and semi-supervised results comparable to supervised learning—and singles out two design choices as especially important: a nonlinear projection head that preserves extra information in h while applying contrastive loss in z, and very large batch sizes that supply many negatives [ts=4137s] [ts=4259s]. MoCo is presented as a remedy for the batch-size bottleneck: it keeps a queue of negative keys across batches, updates a separate key encoder with momentum from the query encoder, and thereby decouples the number of negatives from minibatch size; combining MoCo's queue with SimCLR-style projection heads and augmentations yields a stronger MoCo v2 [ts=4280s] [ts=4414s]. The closing remarks only briefly mention CPC as sequence-level contrastive predictive coding and DINO/MoCo v3 as widely used later frameworks, with DINO noted as student-teacher self-distillation rather than plain contrastive learning [ts=4425s] [ts=4475s].

key_points.text: SimCLR forms positive pairs by sampling two augmentations of the same image and treats all other views in the batch as negatives.
With N input images, SimCLR operates on 2N augmented views, and each view has exactly one positive counterpart in the batch.
A nonlinear projection head is used so contrastive loss acts on z while richer information can remain in h for downstream tasks.
Large batches are important for SimCLR because more negatives improve training, but this increases memory cost.
MoCo replaces in-batch negatives with a running queue of keys and updates the key encoder through momentum.
CPC is mentioned as sequence-level contrastive predictive coding, and DINO as a student-teacher feature-learning framework rather than plain contrastive learning.

kp_catalog.name: SimCLR and MoCo design tradeoffs
kp_catalog.description: SimCLR uses in-batch negatives and a projection head, while MoCo uses a momentum-updated key encoder and a queue to reduce batch-size dependence.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because simCLR and MoCo design tradeoffs clarifies simCLR uses in-batch negatives and a projection head, while MoCo uses a momentum-updated key encoder and a queue to reduce batch-size dependence, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices.

kp_catalog.name: Contrastive learning and InfoNCE
kp_catalog.description: A general SSL formulation that attracts positive pairs and repels negatives, optimized with the InfoNCE objective.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because contrastive learning and InfoNCE formalizes how a general SSL formulation that attracts positive pairs and repels negatives, optimized with the InfoNCE objective, which later units use as core machinery rather than background context.

kp_catalog.name: CPC and DINO as broader SSL directions
kp_catalog.description: CPC extends contrastive learning to sequences, while DINO uses a student-teacher self-distillation setup related to but distinct from plain contrastive learning.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because cPC and DINO as broader SSL directions extends the core thread to cPC extends contrastive learning to sequences, while DINO uses a student-teacher self-distillation setup related to but distinct from plain contrastive learning without being required for the main pipeline.
